items.passage: The human immune system defends the body against harmful bacteria, viruses, and other invading microorganisms. Special white blood cells patrol the bloodstream, constantly searching for anything that seems unusual or dangerous. When a threat appears, these alert cells release chemical signals that summon reinforcements to the site. Some cells even remember past invaders, allowing the body to respond much faster during a second attack. This lasting memory explains why vaccines can protect people for many years.

Permafrost is frozen ground that has remained solid for thousands of years across the northern Arctic. As global temperatures steadily rise, this ancient frozen ground begins to thaw and slowly break apart. Melting releases trapped carbon and methane, powerful gases that warm the atmosphere even further. The softening soil also damages roads, buildings, and pipelines that were built upon the once-stable surface. Scientists monitor these remote frozen regions closely because their changes affect the entire planet.

People often change their opinions and actions to match the behavior of the surrounding social group. This tendency, widely known as conformity, shapes countless everyday decisions in powerful and surprising ways. When individuals feel uncertain, they frequently look to others for guidance about the correct response. Group pressure can quietly push someone to agree with a view they privately doubt. Understanding conformity helps explain how fashions, rumors, and popular beliefs spread through a large community.

Ancient Egyptian doctors developed surprisingly advanced medical practices thousands of years before modern scientific methods appeared. They recorded their treatments on papyrus scrolls, describing remedies for wounds, fevers, and other common problems. Many of their cures combined natural herbs with prayers directed toward powerful healing gods. Skilled surgeons carefully set broken bones and cleaned deep injuries to prevent dangerous infections. This early knowledge later influenced Greek doctors who built upon these valuable written records.

Limestone caves form slowly as slightly acidic rainwater dissolves the solid rock deep beneath the surface. Over thousands of years, the flowing water carves winding passages and enormous hollow underground chambers. Dripping mineral-rich water gradually builds stalactites that hang downward from the shadowy cave ceiling. Below them, matching stalagmites slowly rise upward as the same droplets deposit their dissolved minerals. Together these formations create the strange landscapes hidden within many quiet caves.

Black holes are strange regions of space where gravity becomes so powerful that nothing can escape. They form when massive dying stars collapse inward under their own tremendous crushing weight. The invisible boundary surrounding a black hole is called the event horizon, a point of no return. Because light itself cannot escape, these mysterious objects remain completely invisible to ordinary telescopes. Astronomers detect them only by observing how nearby stars and glowing gas behave.
